tools.name: chat
tools.description:
AI-powered tool for company knowledge. Synthesizes information from multiple sources with intelligent analysis.

Use for:
- Debugging issues, investigating unknown information, or complex questions
- When you need contextual understanding and analysis, not just raw data
- Getting comprehensive answers with reasoning and connections
- Asking follow-up questions or building on previous context

BEST for: Complex queries requiring interpretation, analysis, or synthesis across multiple sources.
CHOOSE search instead for: Simple document retrieval or when you need exact unprocessed results.
SCOPE: AI-powered analysis and synthesis - NOT for simple document retrieval.

context.description: Optional previous messages for context. Will be included in order before the current message

message.description: The user question or message to send to Glean Assistant

tools.name: read_document
tools.description:
This tool can be used to retrieve the full content of one or more URLs
- These URLs could be company internal resources: everything a company uses to communicate and collaborate: documents, messages, emails, etc.
- They could also be external / public resources and web pages
- Results include the corresponding document structure (assuming the tool can successfully read the URL).
- If you need to read multiple URLs, do NOT create separate steps that retrieve each individual URL. Create one step that retrieves all URLs at once.
- Results are always all-or-nothing: the full document or an empty response.
- Do NOT re-read URLs or their variations; the results will be identical.
- An empty string or JSON does not necessarily imply the user's desired document does not exist at all within Glean.

mode.description:
Controls the response format. Omit or leave empty for rich structured data (default).
Set to "raw_bytes" to retrieve the original document bytes instead of extracted text.
For eligible filetypes (O365 xlsx), always attempt "raw_bytes" first — the native file preserves structure, formulas, and formatting that extracted text loses.

If a "raw_bytes" request fails, retry the same call without the mode parameter to fall back to the default structured-text response.

urls.description: A list of URLs to read.

tools.name: search
tools.description:
This is your PRIMARY tool to access all knowledge within the company.
- Use this tool to resolve ambiguities and gather context about company-specific projects, acronyms, and terms.
- By default, the results returned are not exhaustive, we only return the top few most relevant results to a query.
- Do NOT use date filters if the user requests the "latest" or "recent" documents or information but hasn't provided a specific time frame.
- This tool uses a keyword-matching search engine. **Queries MUST be a SHORT sequence of highly targeted, discriminative keywords.**
- **AVOID query stuffing**: Do not add synonyms, categories, or descriptive verbs. If the user asks "what is X", query X.
- AVOID full sentences, duplicate terms, and quotes in the query UNLESS the user is looking for an exact match or verbatim errors.
- Do NOT use boolean logic (OR/AND) in any query.

after.description: filter to documents updated after this date (exclusive). Do not use a date later than today. value must be in "YYYY-MM-DD" format. ONLY when the user has mentioned a specific time frame (e.g. last week, past month, before 12 Jan)

app.description:
filter to documents created in a specific app. Only use the app filter when the user wants contents from an app.
For reference, "salescloud" is an app that stores customer and prospect (leads, contacts, accounts) data, sales data (dashboards, campaigns, opportunities), and interaction history from Salesforce, "career ladder" is described as "Search Contentful job roles and career paths".

before.description: filter to documents updated before this date (exclusive). value must be in "YYYY-MM-DD" format. ONLY when the user has mentioned a specific time frame (e.g. last week, past month, before 12 Jan)

channel.description: filter to documents in a specific channel. Only use this when the user explicitly wants contents from a channel.

dynamic_search_result_filters.description: After your first search, examine each result’s matching_filters. To narrow results, rerun the search using only those discovered filters in dynamic_search_result_filters, formatted as key:value pairs joined by | (e.g., brand:Apple|screen_size:14-inch). This ensures complete coverage of items matching that filter. Never invent filters; use only keys/values seen in results. Do not duplicate those values in the main query.

exhaustive.description: return exhaustive results for this query. Only use if the user requests an exhaustive list of results ("all", "each", "every", ...), or if they're looking for all results that match some filter (e.g. in a time range). Do NOT use this to attempt to fetch more results for a previously issued query.

from.description: filter to documents updated by or commented on or created by this person. Value can be a person's name, "me" or "myteam". Do NOT use other team names, only "myteam" is supported

owner.description: filter to documents created by this person. Value can be a person's name, "me" or "myteam". Do NOT use other team names, only "myteam" is supported

query.description: important keywords that help find relevant documents. Use * if you want to search for all documents satisfying some filters.

sort_by_recency.description: return results sorted by recency (newest first). WARNING: High-risk. Default is relevance. Use ONLY when the user's primary intent is 'latest' or 'most recent' info ('recent launch', 'last message', etc.). Do NOT use this to attempt to fetch more results for a previously issued query.

type.description: filter to documents of a specific type. Only use the type filter when the user wants documents of a specific type.

updated.description: filter to documents updated on or after this date, value can be one of ["today", "yesterday", "past_week", "past_2_weeks", "past_month", "March", ...]. ONLY when the user has mentioned a specific time frame (e.g. last week, past month, before 12 Jan)

tools.name: get_products_composition_batch
tools.description: Retrieve composition, nutritional, and allergen data for products by ID (from batch_search_products). Always call before comparing ingredients, nutrition, or allergens.

tools.name: batch_search_products
tools.description: Search products by keyword, filters, or recommendations — up to 4 queries at once. Use for finding products, gifts, price comparisons, availability checks. Example queries: 'haben Sie Obst', 'günstigster Joghurt', 'empfiehl mir einen Wein', 'empfiehl mir einen Snack', 'Geschenk für Mama', 'teuerste', 'günstigste', 'Bio-Lebensmittel für Kinder', 'Angebote ohne Palmöl', 'Neuheiten', 'vegan'. IMPORTANT: `queries` is the ONLY top-level parameter. Each query object inside `queries` contains ALL search parameters: `keyword`, `filters`, `sort_type`, `include_nutritions`, `include_allergens`, `include_ingredients`, `include_fields`. There are NO top-level parameters other than `queries`. Example: queries=[{keyword: 'milk', include_ingredients: true}, {keyword: 'bread'}]. Returns up to 5 results per query. For more than 4 items, call this tool multiple times.

queries.description: List of up to 4 search queries.

items.description: Single search query within a batch request.

keyword.description: Search keyword for this query. Keywords must be localized for the tenant. Ex: {EXAMPLES_KEYWORD}

filterSlug.description: Type of the filter. (i.e. slozeni, bez-alergenu, options, ...)

valueSlug.description: Value of the filter. (i.e. neobsahuje-palmovy-olej, neobsahuje-mleko, news, ...)

filters.description:
Product filters with filterSlug and valueSlug. Wrong filters return available ones. 
format: `<example filter>: [example values for given filter]`
news: ['news']
sales: ['sales']
kategorie: ['5-broccoli-kohl', '16-bananen-exoten', '29-kochen-backen', '115-getraenke', '532-kuehlregal', '4668-suesses-salziges', '773-haushalt-buero', '833-baby-kinder', '712-drogerie', '663-wurst-schinken']
marke: ['3bears', 'actimel', 'activia', 'aigner', 'airwaves', 'airwick', 'alnatura', 'alnavit', 'alpro', 'alviana']
land: ['aegypten', 'argentinien', 'aethiopien', 'not_in_eu', 'australien', 'barbados', 'belgien', 'bolivien', 'bosnia_und_herzegovina', 'brasilien']

include_nutritions.description: Add nutritional values (energyKJ, energyKCal, fats, carbs, protein, salt).

include_allergens.description: Add allergen info (contained and possibly contained allergens).

include_fields.description: Extra fields to include beyond defaults: vivino (wine ratings), maxBasketAmount, imgPath, recipes, coo (country of origin)

tools.name: get_discounted_items
tools.description: Get currently discounted items. To search for specific products on sale by name, use batch_search_products with sales filter instead.

sale_type.description: Type of sale/discount. 'sales' = all deals, 'premium-sales' = Xtra subscribers deals, 'multipack' = buy more pay less, 'favorite-sales' = popular deals. Recommended: use 'sales' and 'premium-sales' for best deals overview.

category_id.description: Food category ID to filter by.If omitted, returns discounted items across all categories.

limit.description: Maximum number of products to return (1-50, default: 14).

list_categories.description: If true, returns available sales categories instead of products. Use in absence of category info.

tools.name: get_product_details
tools.description: Fetch current details for multiple products at once by their IDs. Returns productId, name, brand, textualAmount, prices, and (by default) the full product description. Set include_description=false to skip fetching descriptions.

product_ids.description: List of product IDs to fetch. Ex: [1234567, 7654321]

include_description.description: If true, also fetches full product description. Default true.

tools.name: get_cart
tools.description: View everything currently in the shopping cart with prices, quantities, and total cost. Use for reviewing before checkout, checking what's already added, calculating totals, or identifying savings from current promotions.

tools.name: update_cart_item
tools.description: Change the quantity of a product already in the cart. Use when the user wants more or fewer of an already-added item, or to reduce amounts before checkout.

tools.name: remove_cart_item
tools.description: Take a specific product out of the cart entirely. Use when the user changes their mind about an individual item or wants to remove something specific before ordering. For removing ALL items at once, use clear_cart instead — do NOT call this repeatedly to empty a cart.

product_id.description: Product ID to remove from cart. Ex: 1234567

tools.name: clear_cart
tools.description: Empty the entire cart at once, removing all items. Use only when the user explicitly wants to remove ALL items — phrases like 'remove everything', 'empty my cart', 'start over', or 'clear the whole cart'. Do NOT use for partial cleanup or ambiguous requests like 'clean up my cart a bit'. Cannot be undone. For removing a single specific item, use remove_cart_item instead.

tools.name: add_items_to_cart
tools.description: Add products to the shopping cart for later checkout. Items added here are NOT part of any existing order and will NOT be delivered until the customer completes checkout. Use after finding products through search, from favorites, recipe ingredients, or shopping lists. Accepts multiple items at once for efficiency. Always confirm: item was added to cart, not to an order.

items.description: Items to add with product_id and quantity. Ex: [{'productId': 1234567, 'quantity': 2}]

source.description: Source identifier for the cart item

tools.name: get_user_addresses
tools.description: List saved delivery addresses for the user. Use when the user needs to select a delivery address during checkout, when discussing delivery options, or when the user wants to see their saved locations.

tools.name: select_delivery_address_checkout
tools.description: Select a delivery address for checkout from the user's saved addresses. Requires address_id from get_user_addresses response. Sets the address on the cart so checkout can proceed. Call get_user_addresses first to list available addresses and let the user choose.

address_id.description: ID of the saved delivery address to select. Get available IDs from get_user_addresses tool.

tools.name: get_user_info
tools.description: Retrieve user's account profile and Xtra membership details.

tools.name: fetch_orders
tools.description: Retrieve past/upcoming orders with details (max 15 orders). REQUIRED: provide at least one of order_id, limit, date_from, or date_to — calling without any parameter will fail. Parameter selection: TIME PERIODS ('last year', 'December') → use date_from + date_to (YYYY-MM-DD). RECENT COUNT ('last 3 orders') → use limit. SPECIFIC ORDER → use order_id. Set order_type to delivered/upcoming/both. Returns full order details with warranty data (disabledData - items that can't be claimed, enabledData - items that can be claimed). Both enabledData and disabledData contain products that were in the order. Use for reordering, claims, shopping lists creation, spending tracking.

order_id.description: Order ID to search for. If used, limit and date parameters are ignored.

limit.description: Number of most recent orders (1-15). Use for count queries ('last 3 orders'). Do not use for time periods ('last year') - use date parameters instead.

date_from.description: Start date (YYYY-MM-DD). Use for time period queries: 'last year', 'December', 'summer', etc. Calculate from today's date (e.g., 'last year' = Jan 1 of previous year). Cannot equal date_to.

date_to.description: End date (YYYY-MM-DD). Use for time period queries: 'last year', 'December', 'summer', etc. Calculate from today's date (e.g., 'last year' = Dec 31 of previous year). Use with date_from.

order_type.description: Type of orders to fetch: 'delivered' for past delivered orders, 'upcoming' for active/upcoming orders, 'both' for all orders. Default is 'delivered'.

tools.name: repeat_order
tools.description: Add all items from previous order to current cart. Use for repeat purchases. Needs order_id from order history.

tools.name: analyze_spending
tools.description: Analyze user spending patterns. Returns total spend, order count, average order value, monthly breakdown, and trends. Use date_from/date_to for time periods (YYYY-MM-DD format).

date_from.description: Start date (YYYY-MM-DD). For 'last year' use Jan 1 of previous year. For 'this year' use Jan 1 of current year. Defaults to 3 months ago if not specified.

date_to.description: End date (YYYY-MM-DD). For 'last year' use Dec 31 of previous year. For 'this year' use today. Defaults to today if not specified.

tools.name: get_typical_order
tools.description: Retrieves frequent items and builds an average order from user purchase history. When add_to_cart=true, adds a median-priced average users order to cart (SIDE EFFECT — do NOT call twice). ALWAYS use for user queries about their 'usual order' or item buying patterns.

tools.name: get_alternative_timeslots
tools.description: Get detailed timeslot availability for existing order including both available and unavailable slots with restriction reasons (EXPIRATION, SOLD_OUT_PRODUCT, SUPPLY_INTERRUPTION, etc). Returns all timeslots across all days with availability status and detailed restriction information. Use when rescheduling existing upcoming order.

order_id.description: Order ID to get detailed timeslot availability for. Ex: 12345678

include_15min_slots.description: Whether to include 15-minute slots. If False, only 1-hour slots are returned.

include_no_capacity_slots.description: Whether to include slots with no capacity. If False, slots with zero capacity are filtered out.

tools.name: change_order_timeslot
tools.description: Changes the delivery timeslot for a confirmed upcoming order (NOT related to the shopping cart). Change delivery timeslot for existing order to any available day (not limited to same day). PREREQUISITE: call get_alternative_timeslots first to get valid slot_id and slot_type. Types: EXPRESS (fast), ON_TIME (scheduled window), VIRTUAL (flexible). Match exact customer time - whole-hour slots differ from quarter-hour slots.

order_id.description: Order ID to change timeslot for. Ex: 12345678

slot_id.description: Timeslot ID to change to. Get this from get_alternative_timeslots response.

slot_type.description: Type of the timeslot. Must be one of: EXPRESS, ON_TIME, VIRTUAL. Get this from get_alternative_timeslots response.

tools.name: get_user_credits
tools.description: Check available credits and vouchers. Use when customer asks about credits, applying credits, credit balance, or discounts or before checkout. Shows balance, restrictions, expiration. Credits are automatically applied at checkout when available.

tools.name: get_all_user_favorites
tools.description: See all products the user has marked as favorites. Use for quick reordering of beloved products, understanding taste preferences, or suggesting similar items the user might enjoy.

tools.name: get_user_shopping_lists_preview
tools.description: See all user's saved shopping lists with names and item counts. Use as first step when working with shopping lists - shows what lists exist before viewing details or adding items.

tools.name: get_user_shopping_list_detail
tools.description: Get shopping list details with products, prices, and stock. Use after preview to see list contents.

tools.name: add_products_to_shopping_list
tools.description: Add one or more products to an existing shopping list — up to 15 at once. Pass all items in the `products` array. Call directly when user says 'add [items]' in a shopping list context; don't confirm list vs cart.

items.description: Single product to add to a shopping list.

tools.name: remove_products_from_shopping_list
tools.description: Remove one or more products from an existing shopping list — up to 15 at once. Pass all product IDs in the `product_ids` array. Use when user wants to delete specific items from their list.

tools.name: delete_shopping_list
tools.description: Delete an entire shopping list. Use when user wants to remove a list they no longer need.

tools.name: create_shopping_list
tools.description: Start a new empty shopping list with a custom name. Use before adding products to the list when user wants to create a new list.

tools.name: get_checkout
tools.description: Get complete checkout form data with all sections and validation status. Returns address, payment, timeslot, packaging, cart total, and validation warnings. Use to review checkout before finalizing.

tools.name: submit_checkout
tools.description: Submit the checkout to complete the order. Submits the current checkout with all selected options (address, payment method, timeslot, etc.) and any required agreement confirmations to finalize the order. Returns submission result with status, messages, and order completion data.

tools.name: change_checkout_packaging
tools.description: Change packaging for checkout. Switch between PAPER_BAGS and REUSABLE_BAGS (eco-friendly, deposit refunded on return). Set before order submit.

selected_packaging.description: Packaging type to select. Options: PAPER_BAGS, REUSABLE_BAGS (more eco-friendly, but requires deposit if the user doesn't have Xtra membership)

tools.name: update_payment_method_checkout
tools.description: Update payment method and/or toggle credit usage before checkout submit. AI agent orders: only CARD_ON_PLACE or CASH allowed (courier payment). Online methods require website.

selected_payment_method.description: Payment method to select. Options: CARD_ON_PLACE (pay by card to courier), CASH (pay cash to courier). Note: CASH is NOT available for DE and AT tenants.

use_credits.description: True: apply credits first; charge remainder to selected method. False: ignore credits; charge full amount to selected method.

tools.name: get_timeslots_checkout
tools.description: Get available delivery timeslots for specific day. Use when looking for a timeslot to place a NEW order to. Returns time windows with prices, express slots, and capacity. Use timeslots_day: 0=today, 1=tomorrow, 2=day after, 3=three days out.

include_15min_slots.description: Whether to include 15-minute slots. If False, only 1-hour slots are returned.

tools.name: change_timeslot_checkout
tools.description: Change selected timeslot during checkout. Requires timeslot_id from get_timeslots_checkout response. Match exact customer time request - whole-hour slots (10:00-11:00) differ from quarter-hour slots (10:00-10:15). Use when selecting a delivery time for a NEW order (it does not reschedule an existing upcoming order).

tools.name: get_recipe_detail
tools.description: Get recipe cooking instructions, ingredient list, and optional product mapping. Use after search to see prep steps and shopping requirements. Example queries: 'wie lange kochen', 'passende Beilage'. Mapping shows matching products with prices.

tools.name: generate_recipe_with_ingredients_search
tools.description: Generate custom recipe and find matching store products. Use for recipes based on products they're buying or personalized meal ideas. Example queries: 'ich möchte einen Schweinebraten kaufen... hast du ein Rezept dafür'. Matched products can be added to cart.

user_query.description: Recipe request. Ex: 'Abendessen für 4 mit Hähnchen'

tools.name: search_recipes_by_vector_similarity
tools.description: Find recipes by ingredients, meal type, or dietary needs. Semantic search. Example queries: 'Rezept mit Brokkoli und Kartoffeln', 'Rezepte mit Schweinefleisch', 'was kann ich aus meiner letzten Bestellung kochen', 'ich habe Spinat im Kühlschrank', 'glutenfrei und laktosefrei', 'Leberdiät', 'Diabetiker', 'Vegetarier', 'Rezeptideen', 'einfaches Gericht', 'Rezepte für zwei Erwachsene'.

query.description: A string describing the recipe you are searching for.

tools.name: get_faq_content
tools.description: Fetch FAQ content for user's region (auto-detected). Returns markdown. Use for questions about store policies, loyalty programs, terms. Pass category slug (general, xtra_general, warranty, returnable_bags, terms_general, etc).

category.description:
FAQ category to fetch. Available categories:
- 'general': General FAQs (orders, delivery, returns, account)
- 'xtra_general': Xtra loyalty program (benefits, points, membership)
- 'xtra_price': Xtra pricing and terms of use
- 'baby_club': Baby club program for parents
- 'christmas': Christmas seasonal information and offers
- 'barrier_free': Barrier-free program — benefits for seniors and pensioners (discounts, special offers, accessibility services)
- 'referral': Referral program information
- 'premium': Premium membership information
- 'returnable_bags': Returnable/eco bags information
- 'office_delivery': Office delivery program
- 'rohlik_point': RohlikPoint pickup locations
- 'gift_vouchers': Gift vouchers/cards
- 'mcp_server': MCP server documentation
- 'farmers_cooperation': Farmer/producer cooperation
- 'partnership': Partnership opportunities
- 'sustainability': Sustainability initiatives
- 'flavor_heroes': Featured products/flavor heroes
- 'terms_general': General terms and conditions
- 'terms_baby_club': Baby club T&C
- 'terms_returnable_bags': Returnable bags T&C
- 'terms_referral': Referral program T&C
- 'terms_xtra_challenge': Xtra challenge T&C
- 'terms_sgr': SGR service T&C
- 'coupon_redemption': Coupon redemption info
- 'warranty': Warranty information
- 'birthday_promo': Birthday promotions
- 'small_producer_cooperation': Small producer cooperation
- 'teamup': Team up/group ordering
- 'career': Career opportunities page

force_refresh.description: Bypass cache and fetch fresh content

tools.name: get_url_content
tools.description: Scrape URL content to markdown. Supports actions (click, wait, scroll) for dynamic pages. Defaults to user's region homepage if no URL. Use for homepage/FAQ/terms/blog/career pages. For job listings use https://career.rohlik.group/group/jobs. Set include_images=true for image URLs (use ![desc](url) in markdown). Cached unless actions used; use force_refresh to bypass.

url.description: URL to scrape and retrieve content from. Supports any valid HTTP/HTTPS URL. If not provided, defaults to the user's region homepage (e.g., https://www.rohlik.cz for Czech users, https://www.knuspr.de for German users, etc.). Examples: FAQ pages, terms of service, about pages, or any public web content.

actions.description: List of actions to perform before scraping. Each action is a dict with 'type' and action-specific params. Supported types: 'click' (requires 'selector'), 'wait' (requires 'milliseconds'), 'scroll' (optional 'direction' or 'selector'), 'write' (requires 'text'), 'press' (requires 'key'). Example: [{'type': 'click', 'selector': '#cookie-accept'}, {'type': 'wait', 'milliseconds': 1000}]. Use for: accepting cookies, navigating menus, loading dynamic content, clicking buttons. Note: Actions bypass caching as they produce dynamic results.

include_links.description: If True, return extracted page links in addition to content. Useful for navigation discovery.

include_html.description: If True, return page HTML structure to discover CSS selectors for actions. HTML is truncated to 50000 characters to prevent token overflow.

include_images.description: If True, return image URLs with descriptions from the page. Image URLs are not cached. Use these URLs to embed images in markdown responses with ![description](url). Images are deduplicated by base URL and limited to max_images.

max_images.description: Maximum number of images to return when include_images=True. Default is 20. Images are deduplicated by base URL to remove size variations. Use lower values (5-10) for homepages, higher values (20-50) for galleries.

force_refresh.description: If True, bypass cache and fetch fresh content from the URL

tools.name: submit_claim
tools.description: Process warranty claims for missing, damaged, or poor quality items. Credits customer account automatically. Each item needs inventoryId, claimedPieces, and reason. Reasons: missing_item, bad_quality, damaged_item, grocery_substitution, bad_expiration_date, bad_weight, pests, other. Match reason to customer's specific complaint.

items.description: List of items to claim, each containing inventoryId, claimedPieces, and reason
Individual item for warranty claim submission.

gave_up_credits.description: Whether customer wants to give credits to charity

tools.name: submit_credit_compensation
tools.description: Compensate customers for collateral damage when one damaged product affects others (e.g., spilled liquid ruining nearby items). NOT for the damaged item itself (use submit_claim for that). Each product needs inventoryId and productName. Severity: Low (minor stain), Medium (partially ruined), High (completely destroyed). Default: Medium.

products.description: List of products affected by damage spread

items.description: Product affected by damage spread for credit compensation.

tools.name: get_customer_care_workflow_prompt
tools.description:
Get step-by-step internal guidelines for handling customer care scenarios that require special procedures. Call this ONLY for claims, warranty issues, returnable bags, cancellations, or order modifications — NOT for regular shopping operations like checkout or product search.

Required inputs:
- workflow_name (string): Name of the workflow. Must be one of:
  - "warranty_claim_processing": For handling missing items, quality issues, damaged items
  - "returnable_bags_issue": For processing returnable bags problems
  - "returnable_packages_credit": For crediting returned bottles/crates not recorded by courier
  - "order_timeslot_change": For changing order delivery timeslot
  - "cancel_order": For handling customer order cancellation requests
  - "checkout": For handling checkout process (only if you need procedural guidance.)
  - "remove_order_items": For removing or reducing quantity of items from upcoming orders
  - "human_transfer_escalation": For escalating customer care issues to a human agent

Example: {"workflow_name": "warranty_claim_processing"}

workflow_name.description: Name of the workflow to retrieve instructions for

tools.name: get_user_reusable_bags_info
tools.description: Check customer's reusable bag count (how many they have at their address). Use for bag count issues (e.g. 'courier didn't scan my bags'). Returns bag tracking counts and deposit info. For deposit refund/money back requests, use cc workflow prompt tool with returnable bags workflow.

tools.name: adjust_user_reusable_bags
tools.description: Correct reusable bag count when tracking is inaccurate. Ask the customer their bag count before calling this — do not infer or assume. Only adjusts bag count — does not trigger deposit refund. For deposit refunds, consult get_customer_care_workflow_prompt with workflow_name='returnable_bags_issue'.

number_of_reusable_bags.description: New number of reusable bags at user's home

description.description: Required free-text explanation when reason_type is 'OTHER_REASON'

tools.name: get_customer_support_contact_info
tools.description: Provide customers with ways to reach human support. Returns phone, email, WhatsApp contact details, operational hours, and is_currently_open indicating whether support is open right now.

tools.name: email_support_on_user_behalf
tools.description: Send an email to customer support on the customer's behalf summarizing their issue. Use when the customer prefers offline email resolution instead of waiting for live support.

issue_summary.description: Summary of the customer's issue composed by Maia

language_code.description: Optional customer language code for internal context (included in email body): cs, hu, de, en, ro. Defaults to en.

order_id.description: Optional order ID if the customer referenced a specific order

tools.name: cancel_order
tools.description:
Cancels a confirmed upcoming delivery order (NOT the shopping cart). Cancel upcoming order (before picking starts). DESTRUCTIVE - requires two-step confirmation flow:
1. INQUIRY ('Can I cancel?') → call with customer_confirmed=false → returns cancellation terms/conditions
2. CONFIRMED ('Yes, cancel it') → call with customer_confirmed=true → executes cancellation
NEVER set customer_confirmed=true without prior explicit user confirmation. Not for ASAP/delivered/picking-started orders. Fetch upcoming orders first if order_id unknown.

customer_confirmed.description: Whether customer has EXPLICITLY confirmed cancellation. Set False for inquiries ('can I cancel?'), True only after explicit confirmation ('yes, cancel it').

tools.name: remove_order_items
tools.description:
Removes items from a confirmed upcoming delivery order (NOT from the shopping cart). Batch remove or reduce quantities of multiple items from an upcoming order (before picking starts). DESTRUCTIVE - requires two-step confirmation flow:
1. INQUIRY → call with customer_confirmed=false → returns all item details and terms for review
2. CONFIRMED → call with customer_confirmed=true → executes removals sequentially with progress updates
NEVER set customer_confirmed=true without prior explicit user confirmation. Fetch order details first to get order_field_id values. Set updated_amount to 0 to remove completely, or to a lower number to reduce quantity. Items are processed one at a time; individual failures do not stop the batch.

items.description: List of items to remove, each with order_field_id and updated_amount
Individual item for batch order item removal.

orderFieldId.description: Order field ID of the item to remove

updatedAmount.description: New quantity for the item (0 to remove completely)

tools.description:
List alerts with optional filters and pagination. Use alert_show for full details on a specific alert.

Each result includes: title, status, alert source (with type), priority, deduplication key, and links.

Filters (all optional):
- query: search alert titles and descriptions
- status: one or more of firing, resolved
- alert_source: one or more alert source config IDs (use alert_source_list to discover these)
- priority: one or more priority IDs (see config://organisation resource for IDs)
- deduplication_key: exact match on a deduplication key
- attributes: filter by alert attribute values (object mapping attribute ID to array of values, see config://organisation for attribute IDs)
- created_after / created_before: dates to bound the time range (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)
- has_related_incident: true for alerts attached to an incident, false for unattached

By default, results are limited to alerts created in the last 30 days. Set created_after to look further back, but prefer a narrow time range alongside other filters — wide ranges on large orgs are slower and more likely to time out.

By default, alerts caught by maintenance windows are excluded from results.

Pagination: results are ordered newest first. Use page_size (default 15, max 50) and pass "next_cursor" as "after" to fetch subsequent pages. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.

Tip: if you need counts, trends, or noise analysis, use alert_stats first — it's much more efficient than paginating through alert_list. Use alert_list when you need to browse specific alerts or inspect their details.

Understanding alert attributes: alerts carry structured attributes (e.g. team, service, environment, feature) that describe what they relate to. Read config://organisation to see what attributes are configured, then use the attributes filter to slice alerts by these dimensions. For example, filtering by service helps answer "which alerts fire for the payments service?" or by team for "what's the alert load for the platform team?"

tools.description:
Count alerts grouped by one or more dimensions, returning aggregate statistics.

Use this tool instead of paginating through alert_list when you need counts, breakdowns, or trend data across many alerts — for example "how many alerts by source this week?" or "what proportion of alerts result in incidents?"

Dimensions (combine freely in the group_by array):
- status: group by status (firing, resolved)
- priority: group by alert priority (e.g. Critical, Urgent, In-hours)
- source: group by alert source name (e.g. Sentry, Grafana, Datadog)
- has_incident: group by whether the alert is attached to an incident (true/false)
- team: group by the org's configured team. Resolves the team alert attribute, so its counts agree with filtering alerts by team. Multi-value: alerts tagged with several teams are counted under each; untagged alerts show as "None".

Scoping to a team: use the team filter for exact teams, or team_part_of to include a parent team and all its sub-teams in the hierarchy (e.g. a "Go To Market" parent covering Sales, Marketing, etc.). Combine team_part_of with group_by ["team"] to break a parent down into its sub-teams.
- attribute:<attribute_id>: group by any alert attribute's value (e.g. team, service, environment). Read config://organisation to find attribute IDs. Array attributes are flattened so each value is counted individually; catalog-typed attributes are resolved to names.
- week: group by ISO week (e.g. 2026-W03)
- month: group by calendar month (e.g. 2026-01)
- quarter: group by calendar quarter (e.g. 2026-Q1)

Each group includes a count, a workload breakdown (responder time from linked incidents, split by working hours, late evening, and overnight), and sample alert IDs that you can pass to alert_show for detail. This lets you answer "which alert sources generate the most on-call work?" directly.

Note on workload scope: workload is summed from all incidents linked to the alerts in each group, regardless of when those incidents were created. This means an alert created in December linked to an incident from November will include November's workload. This is intentional — it measures the total impact generated by the alert — but means alert workload totals may differ from incident_stats workload for the same date range.

By default, alerts caught by maintenance windows are excluded.

The tool processes at most 25,000 alerts. If more match, the response sets "truncated": true — narrow the date range for accurate results.

Conversion rate: to calculate what proportion of alerts become incidents, group by ["source", "has_incident"]. For each source, conversion rate = count(has_incident=true) / total count. Sources with low conversion rates are noise candidates.

Cross-reference: use incident_stats(group_by: ["alert_source"]) to see how alert sources map to incident volume and workload over time. This traces the full path from alert noise through to responder cost. Note that alert counts will be higher than incident counts for the same source, since multiple alerts can link to the same incident.

Deeper analysis: many organisations have a small number of alert sources (e.g. one Datadog integration) but rich alert attributes (team, service, environment, feature). Group by attribute:<attribute_id> to break volume, conversion and workload down by these — for example group_by ["attribute:<service_id>", "has_incident"] to find the noisiest services, or group_by ["team"] for per-team alert load. Read config://organisation to discover attribute IDs. Use alert_list when you need to browse the individual alerts behind a number.

Structured analysis: for a comprehensive report with playbooks, themes, and recommendations, call analysis_start first — it provides step-by-step methodology and a branded report template. Use this tool directly only for quick, focused queries.

group_by.description: dimensions to group by. Categorical: status (firing/resolved), priority, source. Team: team (the org's configured team alert attribute). Attribute: attribute:<attribute_id> for any alert attribute (e.g. team, service, environment). Time buckets: week, month, quarter. Boolean: has_incident (whether alert is attached to an incident). If empty, returns a single total count.

tools.description:
Ask the telemetry-focused AI agent a question, or run a native query verbatim. This agent queries logs, metrics, traces, and dashboards across your connected observability platforms (Datadog, Grafana, Splunk, Honeycomb, etc.). Read the telemetry://datasources resource to see which platforms are connected.

There are two modes:

1. Natural language (default): set "question" and let the agent plan and translate the query. Use this to correlate incident data with observability signals — for example "what do the error rates look like for the payments service?", "show me the logs around the time of INC-123", or "query the CPU dashboard for the last hour".

2. Passthrough (verbatim): set "passthrough" with a complete native expression, its query_type (log_query | metric_query | span_query | sql_query), and the datasource_id to run it against. The expression is the datasource's native query language for log/metric/span (LogQL, PromQL, TraceQL) or a SQL statement for sql_query, and runs exactly as written — the planner is bypassed, nothing reshapes it. Use this when you already hold the precise query (e.g. you authored it from the code, a runbook, or the user). "question" is optional in this mode; when supplied it becomes the recorded query's purpose and steers any follow-up analysis. If you're unsure whether the labels, fields or metric names a verbatim expression references exist on the datasource, call telemetry_inspect on the same datasource_id to confirm them first — a typo fails the whole query. You only need it when you lack prior proof the vocabulary is sound: earlier results this session that already surfaced those labels and metrics, or an expression the user handed you verbatim, are proof enough.

Pass session_id from a previous response to continue a multi-turn conversation.

tools.description:
List entries in a catalog type with optional search and pagination.

Catalog entries are instances of a type — for example, entries in a "Service" type might include "Payments API" or "User Auth". Each entry has attribute values defined by the type's schema, and may have aliases (alternative identifiers like EP011 for escalation paths or owner/repo for GitHub repos).

Required: catalog_type_id (the ID or type_name of the catalog type — use catalog_type_list to discover available types).

The response includes the type's attribute schema alongside entries. Attribute values use attribute names as keys. Reference attributes (those pointing to other catalog types) are formatted as "Name (ID)" so you can read the name directly and use the ID to look up the referenced entry via catalog_entry_show if needed.

Pagination: entries are ordered by name (or rank for ranked types). Use page_size (default 25, max 250) and pass "next_cursor" as "after" to fetch subsequent pages. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.

tools.description:
List escalations (also known as "pages") with optional filters and pagination. When a user asks about pages, paging, or who is being paged, use this tool. Use escalation_show for full details on a specific escalation.

Each result includes: title, status, priority, escalation path, linked incident/alert IDs.

Filters (all optional):
- query: search escalation titles
- status: one or more of pending, triggered, acked, resolved, expired, cancelled, snoozed, pending_repeat
- escalation_path: one or more escalation path IDs
- priority: one or more priority IDs (see config://organisation for IDs)
- user: one or more user IDs to find escalations targeting specific people (e.g. "who is being paged" — filter by the user's own ID from the config resource)
- incident: one or more incident IDs to find escalations linked to specific incidents
- alert: one or more alert IDs to find escalations linked to specific alerts
- created_after / created_before: RFC 3339 timestamps to bound the time range

Pagination: results are ordered newest first. Use page_size (default 15, max 50) and pass "next_cursor" as "after" to fetch subsequent pages. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.

tools.description:
List escalation paths configured in this organisation.

Escalation paths define who gets paged and in what order when an alert fires or someone manually escalates. Each path has levels — level 1 is paged first, then level 2 if nobody acknowledges, and so on. Each path has a short_id (e.g. EP011) that serves as a human-readable alias.

Use escalation_path_show to see the full ladder with who is currently on call at each level. You can search by name or short_id.

Pagination: results are ordered by name. Use page_size (default 15, max 50) and pass "next_cursor" as "after" to fetch subsequent pages. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.

tools.description:
Look up an escalation path and show who would be paged at each level.

Shows the full escalation ladder, resolving schedules to their current on-call users. When the path has conditional branches (e.g. different paths for different priorities or working hours), all branches are shown with the currently active branch marked.

Level 1 is paged first — if nobody acknowledges within the time-to-ack window, level 2 is paged, and so on.

Use this to answer questions like "who would get paged if this alert fires?", "what does the backend escalation look like?", or "who's first-line on-call for this path?"

Use schedule_show to see the full rotation details for any schedule referenced in the path. Use escalation_stats(group_by: ["path"]) to see paging volume and ack rates for this path over time.

tools.description:
Respond to an active escalation (page) on behalf of the authenticated user.

Use escalation_list to find active escalations, then respond with:
- ack: acknowledge the page — stops it from climbing the escalation path. Use when you or an agent are handling the issue.
- nack: decline the page — the escalation continues to the next level. Use when you cannot handle it.

Returns the updated escalation with its new status.

Requires user authentication (e.g. OAuth) rather than an API key, because the response is attributed to a specific user.

tools.description:
Count escalations (pages) grouped by one or more dimensions, returning aggregate statistics with workload from linked incidents.

Use this tool instead of paginating through escalation_list when you need counts or trend data — for example "how many pages per escalation path this week?" or "what's our ack rate?"

Dimensions (combine freely in the group_by array):
- status: group by outcome (pending, triggered, acked, resolved, expired, cancelled, snoozed). "Cancelled" means the page was automatically cancelled because the underlying alert resolved or was attached to an incident — it is system-initiated, not user-initiated. Exclude cancelled from ack rate calculations.
- priority: group by priority (e.g. Critical, Urgent, In-hours)
- path: group by escalation path name (e.g. "Engineering", "SRE On-call")
- team: group by the team that owns the escalation path. Resolves through escalation path ownership, so its counts agree with filtering escalations by team. A path owned by several teams is counted under each; escalations on a path with no team owner show as "None".
- user: group by the user(s) who were paged. Shows user names. An escalation can target multiple users across different levels, so the same escalation may appear under multiple users. Useful for assessing individual on-call burden.
- time_of_day: group by when the escalation was created in UTC — working_hours (09:00-18:00 Mon-Fri), late_evening (18:00-23:00 any day + weekend daytime 09:00-18:00), or overnight (23:00-09:00)
- week: group by ISO week (e.g. 2026-W03)
- month: group by calendar month (e.g. 2026-01)
- quarter: group by calendar quarter (e.g. 2026-Q1)

Scoping to a team: use the team filter for exact teams, or team_part_of to include a parent team and all its sub-teams in the hierarchy. Both resolve through escalation path ownership. Combine team_part_of with group_by ["team"] to break a parent down into its sub-teams.

Key metrics to derive from the results:
- Ack rate: to calculate, group by ["path", "status"] then compute resolved / (resolved + expired) per path. Low rates indicate paging fatigue or coverage gaps.
- Out-of-hours load: group by time_of_day to see working_hours vs late_evening vs overnight split. Overnight pages with low ack rates are the highest priority to fix.
- Path comparison: group by path to see which teams are paged most
- Workload per group: each group includes responder-hours from linked incidents, split by working hours, late evening, and overnight

Each group includes a count, workload breakdown, and sample escalation IDs that you can pass to escalation_show for detail.

The tool processes at most 25,000 escalations. If more match, the response sets "truncated": true — narrow the date range for accurate results.

Cross-reference: use alert_stats(group_by: ["source"]) to see which alert sources drive the most pages, and incident_stats(group_by: ["severity", "month"]) to see how paging load correlates with incident volume. Together the three stats tools give a full picture: alerts → pages → incidents → workload.

Structured analysis: for a comprehensive report with playbooks, themes, and recommendations, call analysis_start first — it provides step-by-step methodology and a branded report template. Use this tool directly only for quick, focused queries.

group_by.description: dimensions to group by. Categorical: status (pending/triggered/acked/resolved/expired/cancelled/snoozed/pending_repeat), priority, path (escalation path name), user (paged user), team (the team that owns the escalation path), time_of_day (working_hours/late_evening/overnight in UTC). Time buckets: week, month, quarter. If empty, returns a single total count.

status.description: filter by state: pending, triggered, acked, resolved, expired, cancelled, snoozed, pending_repeat

team.description: filter to escalations on paths owned by these team IDs (exact match). Mutually exclusive with team_part_of. Use team_list to discover team IDs.

team_part_of.description: filter to escalations on paths owned by these team IDs including all sub-teams in the hierarchy. Use this to scope to a parent team and everything beneath it. Mutually exclusive with team.

created_after.description: only escalations created after this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

created_before.description: only escalations created before this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

max_escalation_ids_per_group.description: how many escalation IDs to return per group (default 10, max 250). Set to 0 to omit IDs entirely.

sort_groups_by.description: order groups by: alphabetical (the default — by dimension key, so week/month/quarter come back chronological), count (highest first), or workload (highest total responder-minutes first). max_groups keeps the first N groups in the current order, so to get a true top-N (e.g. most-paged paths) pass count or workload — otherwise you get an alphabetical slice that can omit the largest groups.

max_groups.description: limit the number of groups returned (e.g. top 10). Check total_groups in the response to see how many existed before truncation. All groups are returned if omitted.

tools.description:
Submit feedback about the incident.io MCP tools to help us improve them.

After completing a task using the MCP tools, reflect on whether the experience could have been better. Consider:
- Was there data you needed but couldn't get, or had to piece together from multiple calls?
- Did a tool return too much or too little information for your use case?
- Was there a question you couldn't answer without paginating through many results?
- Did you have to work around a limitation (e.g. manually counting, converting units, cross-referencing IDs)?
- Would a different grouping dimension, filter, or sort option have saved significant effort?

Examples of valuable feedback:
- "I wanted to find the top 5 noisiest alert sources but had to call alert_stats then manually rank by workload — a built-in ranking would help"
- "incident_show doesn't include the Slack channel name, only the URL — I needed the name to cross-reference with another system"
- "I couldn't filter escalation_stats by the user who was paged — I wanted to show a specific person's paging load"
- "The workload data was in minutes but I always convert to hours — returning both would save a step"

IMPORTANT: Always ask the user for permission before submitting feedback. Draft your feedback, present it to the user, and only call this tool after they approve. The user may want to refine, add context, or decline to send.

The feedback goes directly to the team building these tools and is stored for analysis. Even small friction points are valuable.

context.description: what were you trying to do? Describe the goal or task that led to this feedback.

what_was_tried.description: what did you try, and what was missing or didn't work? Describe the tools you used and where you hit friction.

ideal_functionality.description: what would make this great? Describe the feature, data, or improvement that would have solved the problem.

tools.name: feedback

status.description: confirmation that the feedback was submitted

tools.description:
Create a follow-up on an incident, either from scratch or by importing an existing external ticket.

Follow-ups are post-incident tasks — work that needs to happen after an incident is resolved, like "add monitoring for X", "update runbook", or "fix the root cause". They are distinct from in-flight actions which are checklist items used during active response. Follow-ups persist, can be assigned, prioritised, labelled, and exported to external issue trackers.

There are two modes:

1. **Create from scratch**: provide incident_id and title (required), plus optional description, assignee_id, priority_id, labels. The follow-up is created with status "outstanding".

2. **Import from external ticket**: provide incident_id and url (a link to an existing Linear, Jira, GitHub, GitLab, Asana, ClickUp, or Shortcut issue). The title, description, status, and assignee are imported from the ticket automatically. The follow-up is linked to the external issue for ongoing sync.

If the org requires a priority or assignee, those fields become required and the tool will return a validation error if they're missing.

title.description: follow-up title describing the action needed (required unless url is provided)

assignee_id.description: user ID to assign the follow-up to

priority_id.description: follow-up priority ID (see config://organisation for IDs)

labels.description: labels to attach (max 50 chars each, created automatically if new)

url.description: URL of an existing issue in an external tracker (Linear, Jira, GitHub, etc.) to import as a follow-up — when provided, title and description are fetched from the ticket automatically

Use this tool when you need counts or breakdowns across many follow-ups — for example "how many outstanding follow-ups by priority?" or "follow-up completion rate by incident severity". Use follow_up_list to browse individual follow-ups.

tools.description:
Create a new incident.

Use this tool when you know what incident to create (e.g. from an alert, a customer report, or a detected issue). For interactive incident declaration with AI assistance, use the ask agent's declare_incident tool instead.

Required: name (a short, descriptive title).

The incident is created in triage by default — this means no severity is required and the team can assess before accepting. To create an already-accepted incident, provide both severity_id and an active incident_status_id.

**Never invent a severity.** Only pass severity_id if the user has explicitly stated one (e.g. "create a sev2", "this is critical"). If they haven't, leave severity_id empty so the incident starts in triage and a responder can set it later.

Some organisations have disabled manual triage incidents — those require severity_id at creation time. The organisation's declare form may also require custom fields on every incident, including triage. Calls missing any required field return a "missing_required_fields" validation error that lists each field by name and includes a link to the declare form, prefilled with what you supplied. Share that link with the user so they can complete the remaining required fields there — don't try to resolve catalog IDs and re-call yourself.

Defaults applied when fields are omitted:
- visibility: public
- incident_type_id: the organisation's default incident type
- incident_status_id: triage (only if the organisation allows manual triage incidents)
- severity_id: none (allowed in triage; required if status is active or if the org has disabled manual triage)
- slack_team_id: only meaningful on Slack Enterprise Grid with multiple workspaces — defaults to the workspace the authenticated user is a member of (when unambiguous), otherwise the organisation's primary workspace

Read the config://organisation resource to discover severity IDs, status IDs, incident type IDs, custom field IDs, and role IDs.

Returns the created incident summary. Use incident_show for full details.

name.description: short, descriptive incident name (e.g. 'Database connection pool exhausted')

summary.description: initial summary describing what is happening (markdown)

severity_id.description: severity ID (see config://organisation). If omitted, the incident starts in triage with no severity — set severity when you accept it.

tools.description:
Search and list incidents, returning a summary per result. Use incident_show to get full details for a specific incident.

Each result includes: reference (e.g. INC-123), name, status (with category), severity, mode, reported_at, updated_at, and a permalink. A merged incident (status category "merged") also includes "merged_into", identifying the incident it was merged into.

Use the include parameter to enrich results with additional fields, avoiding the need to call incident_show for each incident:
- summary: short incident summary (~200 chars, AI-generated or human-written) — useful for understanding what each incident was about without fetching full details
- roles: include role assignments (e.g. incident lead) with user names and IDs
- custom_fields: include custom field values
- timestamps: named timestamps (e.g. reported at, resolved at) — useful for timeline and trend analysis
- durations: duration metrics (e.g. time to resolve, in seconds) — useful for comparing incident response times
- escalation_urgency: include AI assessment of whether each incident needed immediate response (urgency level and tags). Only available for orgs with AI investigations enabled. Use incident_show for the full reasoning text.

This is especially useful for analytical queries like "who has been incident lead the most?" — include roles and paginate through the results rather than calling incident_show for each one. For trend analysis, include summary, timestamps, and durations to get context without extra API calls.

By default only standard and retrospective incidents are returned, and declined/canceled/merged incidents are excluded. Provide explicit filters to override these defaults.

Filters (all optional):
- query: free-text search across incident names
- status_category: one or more of triage, active, post-incident, closed, paused, declined, merged, canceled
- severity: one or more severity IDs
- incident_type: one or more incident type IDs (e.g. filter to "Default" only, or exclude "Internal" and "Maintenance")
- mode: one or more of standard, retrospective, test, tutorial
- team: one or more team IDs (exact match — incidents tagged with any of these teams)
- team_part_of: one or more team IDs including all sub-teams in the hierarchy (e.g. "Platform" returns incidents for Platform and all its child teams). Mutually exclusive with team.
- custom_fields: filter by custom field values (object mapping field ID to array of value IDs)
- created_after / created_before: dates to bound the time range (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)
- assigned_to: one or more user IDs (or the literal "me" for the calling user). Returns incidents where any listed user is a participant (responder/collaborator/observer) or has any incident role assignment (e.g. lead). Use this to triage your own workload without wading through everyone else's, e.g. {"assigned_to": ["me"], "status_category": ["triage", "active"]}. "me" only works for OAuth tokens, not API keys.

To discover severity IDs, incident type IDs, custom field IDs, and custom field value IDs, read the config://organisation resource first.

Sorting: results are ordered newest first by default. Set sort_by to "workload" to order by total responder-minutes (highest first), which is useful for finding the most costly incidents. Workload data is synced hourly from our insights pipeline.

Pagination: use page_size to control the page (default 10, max 50). When the response has "has_more": true, pass the "next_cursor" value as "after" to fetch the next page. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.

Tip: if you need counts or trend data, use incident_stats first — it's much more efficient than paginating through incident_list and counting manually. Use incident_list when you need to browse individual records or need the detail that includes provide.

after.description: cursor from a previous page to fetch the next one

sort_by.description: sort order: created_at (default, newest first) or workload (highest total responder-minutes first, uses insights data synced hourly)

status_category.description: filter by status category: triage, active, post-incident, closed, paused, declined, merged, canceled

incident_type.description: filter by incident type ID (read config://organisation to discover type IDs)

mode.description: filter by mode: standard, retrospective, test, tutorial

team.description: filter by team IDs (exact match). Mutually exclusive with team_part_of. Use team_list to discover team IDs.

team_part_of.description: filter by team IDs including all sub-teams in the hierarchy. Use this when you want incidents for a team and all its descendant teams. Mutually exclusive with team. Use team_list to discover team IDs.

custom_fields.description: filter by custom field values: keys are custom field IDs, values are arrays of accepted value IDs (read the config://organisation resource to discover field and value IDs)

created_after.description: only incidents created after this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

created_before.description: only incidents created before this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

assigned_to.description: filter to incidents where any of these users is involved — either as a participant (responder, collaborator, observer) or assigned to any incident role (lead, etc.). The literal string "me" resolves to the calling user (only available for OAuth tokens, not API keys). Useful for triaging your own workload — e.g. {"assigned_to": ["me"], "status_category": ["triage", "active"]} returns your open incidents. Use the "You" section of config://organisation to find your own user ID, or team_list / team_show to find other users' IDs.

include.description: optional fields to enrich each result with: summary (short incident summary, ~200 chars), roles (role assignments), custom_fields (custom field values), timestamps (named timestamps like reported at, resolved at), durations (duration metrics like time to resolve), escalation_urgency (AI assessment of whether immediate response was needed — urgency and tags only, use incident_show for full reasoning). Use these to avoid calling incident_show for every result.

tools.description:
Look up an incident by ID or reference (e.g. INC-123, 123, or a full ID) and return its details.

Returns: name, status, severity, roles, participants, custom fields, timestamps, durations, workload (responder time split by working/late/overnight hours), summaries, channel URLs, and the 5 most recent status updates. For a merged incident, "merged_into" identifies the incident it was merged into.

Use 'include' to request additional sections:
- investigation: AI investigation findings and extended summary. Always request this when investigating root causes or triaging — it contains the analysis our AI has already performed.
- postmortem: the post-mortem document content. Request this when reviewing what happened or preparing reports — it contains the team's written analysis.
- updates: all status updates (default returns most recent 5 only). Request this when you need the full timeline of how the incident progressed.
- escalation_urgency: AI assessment of whether the incident truly needed immediate response (immediate, deferrable, or unsure) with classification tags and reasoning. Useful for on-call burden analysis and alert noise reviews. Only available for orgs with AI investigations enabled.

For investigation or root cause analysis, request include: ["investigation", "postmortem"] — the default response without these only gives you the basic metadata, not the deeper analysis.

For the full investigation content (all findings, checks, conversation history, and incident context as a file tree), use the investigation_sync tool instead — it returns a downloadable tar.gz archive of the complete investigation filesystem.

tools.description:
Count incidents grouped by one or more dimensions, returning aggregate statistics.

Use this tool when you need counts, breakdowns, or trend data across many incidents — for example "how many P1s this quarter?" or "incident volume by team by month". Use incident_list when you need to browse or search individual incidents, and incident_show to get full details for a specific incident.

Dimensions (combine freely in the group_by array):
- severity: group by severity level (e.g. Critical, Major, Minor)
- status: group by status category (e.g. triage, active, closed)
- type: group by incident type
- mode: group by mode (standard, retrospective)
- alert_source: group by the alert source that triggered the incident (e.g. Datadog, Sentry). Shows "No alert" for manually created incidents. Multi-value: incidents with alerts from multiple sources are counted under each source individually.
- escalation_urgency: group by AI-assessed urgency (immediate, deferrable, unsure, or "Not assessed" for incidents without analysis). Useful for quantifying alert noise and on-call burden — combine with month for trend analysis.
- team: group by the org's configured team (e.g. Engineering, Platform). Prefer this over grouping by a team-shaped custom field (custom_field:<id>) — "team" resolves the org's configured team field, so its counts agree with filtering by team. Multi-value: incidents tagged with several teams are counted under each; incidents with no team show as "None". Combine with month/severity for per-team trends and workload.
- custom_field:<field_id>: group by a custom field's values (read config://organisation to find field IDs). Multi-value: multi-select fields are flattened so each value is counted individually.
- role:<role_id>: group by who is assigned a specific role (e.g. Incident Lead). Shows user names, or "Unassigned" for incidents without that role.
- week: group by ISO week (e.g. 2026-W03)
- month: group by calendar month (e.g. 2026-01)
- quarter: group by calendar quarter (e.g. 2026-Q1)

Each group includes a count, a workload breakdown (total responder-minutes split by working hours, late evening, and overnight), and the most recent incident references (e.g. INC-123) which you can pass directly to incident_show to drill into. Workload data is synced hourly from our insights pipeline and may be absent for very recent incidents.

By default, test and tutorial incidents are excluded and declined/canceled/merged incidents are excluded. Provide explicit status_category or mode filters to override these defaults.

The tool processes at most 25,000 incidents. If more match, the response sets "truncated": true — narrow the date range for accurate results.

Drill-down: each group includes incident references (e.g. INC-123). Pass these to incident_show with include: ["investigation", "postmortem"] to understand root causes behind the numbers.

Cross-reference: compare with alert_stats(group_by: ["source"]) to see how alert volume and noise translate to the incident workload shown here.

Structured analysis: for a comprehensive report with playbooks, themes, and recommendations, call analysis_start first — it provides step-by-step methodology and a branded report template. Use this tool directly only for quick, focused queries.

group_by.description: dimensions to group by, e.g. ["severity", "month"]. Categorical: severity, status, type, mode, escalation_urgency, team. Custom field: custom_field:<field_id>. Time buckets: week, month, quarter. If empty, returns a single total count.

team_part_of.description: filter by team IDs including all sub-teams in the hierarchy. Mutually exclusive with team. Use team_list to discover team IDs.

custom_fields.description: filter by custom field values: keys are custom field IDs, values are arrays of accepted value IDs

created_after.description: only incidents created after this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

created_before.description: only incidents created before this date (yyyy-mm-dd or RFC 3339)

max_incident_ids_per_group.description: how many incident references (e.g. INC-123) to return per group (default 10, max 250). Set to 0 to omit IDs entirely.

sort_groups_by.description: order groups by: alphabetical (the default — by dimension key, so week/month/quarter come back chronological), count (highest first), or workload (highest total responder-minutes first). max_groups keeps the first N groups in the current order, so to get a true top-N (e.g. busiest teams) pass count or workload — otherwise you get an alphabetical slice that can omit the largest groups.

tools.description:
Update an incident. Only the fields you provide will be changed — omitted fields are left as-is.

Optionally include a message to post to the incident channel alongside the change. Use a message when the update deserves an explanation visible to the team (e.g. "Escalating to Critical — customer reports are increasing") or to record a progress note on the timeline. If you're just changing fields without context to share, you can omit the message.

For help deciding what to update or drafting a message, use ask_incident instead.

Fields:
- severity_id: change severity level
- incident_status_id: change status (respects lifecycle rules — see below)
- incident_type_id: change the incident type (only allowed while in triage)
- name: rename the incident
- summary: set or replace the incident summary (markdown)
- custom_field_entries: set custom field values (replaces all values for that field)
- role_assignments: assign or unassign incident roles
- timestamps: set or clear incident timestamps (e.g. when impact started)
- merged_into_incident_id: merge this incident into another (see below)
- message: a status update posted to the incident channel (markdown). This is a point-in-time message visible to the team, distinct from the summary which is the persistent incident description.

Status transitions follow lifecycle rules:
- From triage: can move to any active status (accepting the incident)
- From active: can move to other active statuses, or to post-incident (resolving)
- From post-incident: can move to closed
- Cannot reopen closed, declined, merged, or canceled incidents

Accepting an incident requires an incident type: when moving out of triage, the
incident must have an incident type set. Incidents declared from alerts often have
none. If the incident has no type yet, pass incident_type_id alongside
incident_status_id in the same call (incident_type_id can only be set while still in
triage) — otherwise the update is rejected and no changes are applied.

To merge one incident into another, set incident_status_id to the merged status (see
config://organisation) and merged_into_incident_id to the target incident. The incident
being merged must be in triage, active, or paused. To merge several incidents into one,
call this tool once per source incident, each pointing at the same target.

Read the config://organisation resource to discover severity IDs, status IDs, custom field IDs, role IDs, and timestamp IDs.

After resolving an incident, use follow_up_create to capture post-incident tasks.

id.description: incident to update (ID, external ID, or reference like INC-123)

message.description: status update message posted to the incident channel (markdown). Use this to communicate progress, findings, or decisions to the team.

incident_status_id.description: new status ID (see config://organisation). Moving to a closed status will opt out of the post-incident flow. Moving to a post-incident status will resolve the incident.

incident_type_id.description: new incident type ID (see config://organisation). Can only be set while the incident is in triage. Accepting an incident (moving it out of triage) requires an incident type — if the incident doesn't already have one, set it in the same call as incident_status_id.

merged_into_incident_id.description: the incident to merge this one into (ID, external ID, or reference like INC-123). Required when incident_status_id is set to a merged status. The incident being merged must be in triage, active, or paused.

tools.description:
Record a user's overall feedback on an AI investigation — whether it was helpful in responding to the incident. Feedback can only be submitted once the incident is resolved.

Provide either incident_id (ID, external ID, or reference like INC-123) or investigation_id. When using incident_id, the most recent completed investigation for that incident is selected.

Set sentiment to "helpful" or "not_helpful". For not_helpful, optionally include reasons (not_accurate, not_readable, too_late, too_frequent, missing_information, other) and a comment. A comment can accompany either sentiment.

IMPORTANT: This submits feedback on the user's behalf. Always confirm the sentiment (and any reasons/comment) with the user before calling this tool — don't infer their verdict and submit it unprompted.

incident_id.description: incident ID, external ID, or reference (e.g. INC-123). Resolves to the latest investigation for this incident.

investigation_id.description: specific investigation ID to give feedback on. Use this if you already know the investigation ID.

sentiment.description: overall verdict on the investigation: 'helpful' or 'not_helpful'

reasons.description: only for not_helpful — one or more of: not_accurate, not_readable, too_late, too_frequent, missing_information, other

comment.description: optional freeform comment expanding on the feedback

tools.description:
Feed information into an in-progress investigation so it re-assesses its hypothesis.

Use this when you know something the investigation doesn't — for example, the responder has identified the real cause, telemetry shows a different explanation, or the investigation is going down the wrong path.

Provide either incident_id (ID, external ID, or reference like INC-123) or investigation_id. When using incident_id, the latest investigation is selected.

The directive should explain what the investigation should focus on and why its current hypothesis may be wrong. Good directives reference the current hypothesis and explain the correction:
- "The investigation is focused on Postgres connection pooling, but Redis connections are exhausted (100% since 14:32) — focus on Redis instead"
- "The error started immediately after the 14:30 deploy of payment-service v2.3.1 — the investigation should look at that deploy"
- "Ignore the Sentry errors — they're a known false positive from last week, not related to this incident"

Include evidence (telemetry results, log excerpts, etc.) and telemetry_query_ids when available — the investigation unpacks query IDs into rich artifacts with query expressions, datasource details, and explore URLs.

After steering, the investigation re-assesses its hypothesis within a few minutes (checks are debounced to coalesce rapid updates).

incident_id.description: incident ID, external ID, or reference (e.g. INC-123). Resolves to the latest investigation.

investigation_id.description: specific investigation ID. Use this if you already know the investigation ID.

directive.description: what the investigation should focus on and why, referencing the current hypothesis where possible. Example: 'The investigation is focused on Postgres but Redis connections are exhausted since 14:32 — focus on Redis instead'

evidence.description: supporting data as markdown — telemetry results, log excerpts, conversation context, or anything that backs up the directive

telemetry_query_ids.description: IDs of telemetry queries from ask_telemetry calls. These are unpacked into artifacts with query expressions, datasource info, and explore URLs.

tools.name: investigation_steer

incident_id.description: the incident this investigation belongs to

tools.description:
Download an investigation as a tar.gz archive containing the full investigation filesystem.

Provide either incident_id (ID, external ID, or reference like INC-123) or investigation_id. When using incident_id, the latest investigation is selected.

The archive contains markdown files describing the investigation: findings, checks, incident context, conversation history, and more.

Optionally pass since (a timestamp) to include a preamble showing what changed since that time — useful for polling an in-progress investigation.

Returns a signed URL valid for 15 minutes.

incident_id.description: incident ID, external ID, or reference (e.g. INC-123). Resolves to the latest investigation for this incident.

investigation_id.description: specific investigation ID to download. Use this if you already know the investigation ID.

since.description: if provided, includes a preamble showing what changed since this time (RFC 3339 or any common date format)

tools.name: investigation_sync

download_url.description: signed URL to download the investigation as a tar.gz archive (valid for 15 minutes)

investigation_id.description: the investigation ID that was rendered

tools.description:
Read a resource by name. Resources provide context that helps you use the other tools effectively. This tool returns the same content as the MCP resources, for clients that don't support the resources protocol.

Use resource_list to see all available resources, or pass one of these names:
- alert_noise_playbook: Playbook for deep alert noise analysis. Identifies noisy sources, quantifies noise cost in responder time, and produces specific tuning recommendations.
- analysis_playbook: Index of all analysis playbooks. Describes which playbook to use for different questions (operational review, alert noise, on-call burden, team health, response effectiveness). Read this first to pick the right playbook.
- metrics_glossary: Glossary of derived metrics used across playbooks: workload ratios, alert quality scores, escalation rates, and lifecycle timings. Includes formulas, interpretation guidance, and source tools.
- on_call_burden_playbook: Playbook for assessing on-call sustainability. Analyses paging volume, ack rates, overnight burden, and workload distribution across people and teams.
- onboarding_context: datasource summaries and recent successful telemetry queries
- operational_review_playbook: Playbook for periodic operational reviews (weekly/monthly/quarterly). Covers incident volume, alert noise, paging load, responder burden, and follow-up health in one comprehensive analysis.
- organisation: incident statuses, severities, roles, types, custom fields, alert priorities — discover IDs for filtering
- presenting_results_guide: Guide for structuring and formatting analysis findings. Covers output structure, formatting conventions, and the branded HTML report template.
- response_effectiveness_playbook: Playbook for measuring response effectiveness: detection speed, resolution time, escalation response, and post-incident learning outcomes.
- scope_playbook: How to establish analysis scope with the user before running a playbook. Covers goal, time window, team/org scope, comparison periods, audience, and output format. Run this first.
- team_health_playbook: Playbook for per-team or cross-team operational health assessment. Combines incident, alert, and escalation data scoped to a team.
- telemetry_datasources: connected observability platforms and their capabilities

resource.description: which resource to read — use resource_list to see all available resources

tools.description:
List on-call schedules with optional search and pagination.

Schedules define who is on call and when. Each result shows who is currently on call, when the next handover occurs, and how many rotations the schedule has.

Use schedule_show for full details including rotation configuration and upcoming shifts.

Pagination: results are ordered by name. Use page_size (default 15, max 50) and pass "next_cursor" as "after" to fetch subsequent pages. Stopping before "has_more" is false means you have incomplete results.
